perturbation.original_text:
8. PRODUCT RETURNS
8.1. Procedure. Distributor may return Products to Vendor for credit or full reimbursement, as the case may be, only pursuant to this Section 8.
8.2 Updated Products. Distributor may return to Vendor for credit against future purchases any Products for which a new version or upgrade has been produced and offered for sale; provided, however, the new version or upgrade must be of a material nature whereby the existing Products held by Distributor are considered obsolete. All Products must be returned undamaged, and all shipping charges -7- shall be paid by Distributor. This clause does not apply to any Products that have been in Distributor's inventory for more that one hundred and eighty (180) calendar days.
8.3. Damaged Products. Promptly upon the receipt of a shipment of Products, Distributor shall inspect the Products for damage or shortage. Within ten (10) calendar days of receipt of the shipment, Distributor shall notify Vendor of any damage or shortage. As soon as commercially practical after receipt of notice, Vendor shall make complete any shipment in short supply. Any Products damaged in shipment shall be returned to Vendor along with documentation of the damage within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt by Distributor, and Vendor will reimburse Distributor for the costs of freight reasonably incurred by Distributor in returning the Products to Vendor. If Vendor finds any Products returned for damage to not be damaged, Distributor shall be subject to a restocking fee equal to fifteen percent (15%) original purchase price of the non-damaged Products.
8.4. Defective Products. Distributor may, no later than one hundred and twenty (120) calendar days after a Product is sold by Distributor to a customer, return to Vendor, at Distributor's expense, any Product received by Distributor from Vendor during the 120-day period prior to such return, which Distributor or its customer believes to be defective. In the event that such Product is defective, i.e., the failure of a Product to operate in accordance with its published specifications, Vendor shall:
8.4.1. Reimburse Distributor for the costs of freight reasonably incurred by Distributor in returning the Product to Vendor; and
8.4.2. Issue Distributor a credit against future purchases in an amount equal to the purchase price paid by Distributor for the Product or, as requested by Distributor, full reimbursement for the defective Products.
perturbation.changed_text:
8. PRODUCT RETURNS
8.1. Procedure. Distributor may return Products to Vendor solely at Vendor's discretion.
8.2 Updated Products. Distributor may return to Vendor for credit against future purchases any Products for which a new version or upgrade has been produced and offered for sale; provided, however, the new version or upgrade must be of a material nature whereby the existing Products held by Distributor are considered obsolete. All Products must be returned undamaged, and all shipping charges -7- shall be paid by Distributor. This clause does not apply to any Products that have been in Distributor's inventory for more that one hundred and eighty (180) calendar days.
8.3. Damaged Products. Promptly upon the receipt of a shipment of Products, Distributor shall inspect the Products for damage or shortage. Within ten (10) calendar days of receipt of the shipment, Distributor shall notify Vendor of any damage or shortage. As soon as commercially practical after receipt of notice, Vendor shall make complete any shipment in short supply. Any Products damaged in shipment shall be returned to Vendor along with documentation of the damage within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt by Distributor, and Vendor will reimburse Distributor for the costs of freight reasonably incurred by Distributor in returning the Products to Vendor. If Vendor finds any Products returned for damage to not be damaged, Distributor shall be subject to a restocking fee equal to fifteen percent (15%) original purchase price of the non-damaged Products.
8.4. Defective Products. Distributor may, no later than one hundred and twenty (120) calendar days after a Product is sold by Distributor to a customer, return to Vendor, at Distributor's expense, any Product received by Distributor from Vendor during the 120-day period prior to such return, which Distributor or its customer believes to be defective. In the event that such Product is defective, i.e., the failure of a Product to operate in accordance with its published specifications, Vendor shall:
8.4.1. Reimburse Distributor for the costs of freight reasonably incurred by Distributor in returning the Product to Vendor; and
8.4.2. Issue Distributor a credit against future purchases in an amount equal to the purchase price paid by Distributor for the Product or, as requested by Distributor, full reimbursement for the defective Products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return policy to be solely at Vendor's discretion removes any guaranteed return rights, which is a deviation from standard commercial practices and could potentially contradict consumer protection laws regarding defective or non-conforming goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the implied warranty provisions under the Ontario Consumer Protection Act, specifically related to acceptable quality. By stating returns are 'solely at Vendor's discretion,' it undermines the consumer's right to return defective goods, potentially violating these implied warranties.

perturbation.justification: The original text outlines specific scenarios where the distributor can return products. The changed text replaces this with a clause stating returns are 'solely at Vendor's discretion.' This eliminates the distributor's previously established rights to return products under specific conditions. The Law Explanation states this undermines the consumer's right to return defective goods, potentially violating implied warranties, which relates to consumer protection laws as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. While the snippet does not contain explicit wording on acceptable quality, it references the Consumer Protection Act, suggesting the modified text potentially undermines the consumer's rights.

perturbation.original_text:
23. JURISDICTION
Disputes under this Agreement shall be resolved through arbitration in Ontario, Canada by a single arbitrator to be appointed by agreement of the parties, or in default, by the President of the Law Society/Bar of Canada. The laws of Ontario, Canada shall govern the conduct of the arbitration and any appeal from the decision of the arbitrator.
perturbation.changed_text:
23. JURISDICTION
Disputes under this Agreement shall be resolved through arbitration in Ontario, Canada by a single arbitrator to be appointed by agreement of the parties, or in default, by the President of the Law Society/Bar of Canada. The laws of Vendor's choice shall govern the conduct of the arbitration and any appeal from the decision of the arbitrator.
perturbation.explanation: Changing governing law from Ontario, Canada law to the law of the Vendor's choice permits the vendor to choose a jurisdiction that may not provide adequate protection for the distributor, especially if the vendor's jurisdiction has weaker consumer/distributor protection laws. The Vendor may choose to use international law which often lacks strong protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specification that the laws of Ontario govern the arbitration, the modified text contradicts the spirit of the Arbitration Act, which aims to provide a fair and predictable framework for arbitration within Ontario. Allowing the 'Vendor's choice' of law undermines this framework and may permit the circumvention of Ontario's consumer protection laws during arbitration.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the laws of Ontario, Canada, shall govern the arbitration. The changed text allows the Vendor to choose the governing law, which contradicts the purpose of the Arbitration Act, as the "Law Explanation" states. Allowing the Vendor's choice of law undermines the original framework. The 'Perturbation Explanation' supports this by stating that the Vendor may choose to use international law which often lacks strong protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the Arbitration Act exists. The change removes the application of Ontario law, creating an omission that weakens the protection intended by the original clause.

perturbation.original_text:
12. LIMITATION OF WARRANTIES
Vendor warrants that all Products are new and that, to its knowledge, all Vendor-supplied promotional materials comply in all respects with all applicable laws, rules and regulations. Although Vendor intends to provide a six-month limited warranty to the end user, Distributor shall make no warranties or representations with respect to the Products on behalf of Vendor. Distributor shall defend, indemnify and hold Vendor harmless from any and all claims, damages, costs or expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by Vendor in relation to any violation by Distributor of the foregoing sentence.
perturbation.changed_text:
12. LIMITATION OF WARRANTIES
Vendor warrants that all Products are new. Although Vendor intends to provide a six-month limited warranty to the end user, Distributor shall make no warranties or representations with respect to the Products on behalf of Vendor. Distributor shall defend, indemnify and hold Vendor harmless from any and all claims, damages, costs or expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by Vendor in relation to any violation by Distributor of the foregoing sentence.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating that Vendor-supplied promotional materials comply with applicable laws eliminates an explicit warranty regarding regulatory compliance. This could expose the distributor to liability if the promotional materials are, in fact, non-compliant with local laws where the distributor operates.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the warranty that promotional materials comply with laws, the modified text increases the risk of violating Part III of the Ontario Consumer Protection Act, 2002, which regulates advertising and prohibits false, misleading, or deceptive representations. The Distributor might unknowingly use non-compliant promotional material.
perturbation.justification: The original contract included a warranty that Vendor-supplied promotional materials comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations. The changed text removes this warranty, potentially shifting the burden of ensuring regulatory compliance onto the Distributor. The Law Explanation suggests this omission could lead to violations of consumer protection laws, as the Distributor might unknowingly use non-compliant materials. While there are no scraped law snippets to confirm the violation of a specific law directly, the removal of the explicit warranty concerning legal compliance represents a significant weakening of the original contract's protective measures, creating a loophole where the Distributor becomes vulnerable to liability for unknowingly distributing non-compliant promotional materials.
